perturbation.original_text: You will make reasonable and appropriate efforts to safeguard Evaluation Material from disclosure by you or your Representatives to anyone other than as permitted hereby by using, in all material respects, the same degree of care that you use to protect your own confidential, proprietary information.
perturbation.changed_text: You will endeavor to safeguard Evaluation Material from disclosure by you or your Representatives to anyone other than as permitted hereby, using a level of care deemed adequate by your internal policies.
perturbation.explanation: The phrase 'endeavor to safeguard' weakens the obligation. Replacing 'same degree of care that you use to protect your own confidential, proprietary information' with 'level of care deemed adequate by your internal policies' introduces subjectivity, potentially leading to inadequate protection of confidential information.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New York Personal Information Security Breach and Notification Act
perturbation.law_explanation: New York law requires reasonable security measures to protect private information. The modified text's 'level of care deemed adequate by your internal policies' is ambiguous and might not meet the 'reasonable security measures' standard, potentially leading to non-compliance if internal policies are lax or inadequate, increasing the risk of a data breach and subsequent violations of the notification requirements.
perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the obligation to safeguard Evaluation Material by changing "You will make reasonable and appropriate efforts to safeguard...by using, in all material respects, the same degree of care that you use to protect your own confidential, proprietary information" to "You will endeavor to safeguard Evaluation Material...using a level of care deemed adequate by your internal policies." The law explanation argues this might not meet the 'reasonable security measures' standard required by New York law, especially if internal policies are lax. Scraped Law Snippet 1, from NYS General Business Law Article 39-F Section 899-BB, pertains to 'Data security protections'. While the snippet itself doesn't specify 'reasonable security measures,' its very existence indicates a legislative focus on data protection standards. The shift to 'endeavor' and 'level of care deemed adequate by your internal policies' introduces ambiguity, potentially contradicting the implicit requirement for reasonable data security measures that New York law aims to enforce, making the obligation ambiguous, and thus creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: If you or your Representatives are requested or required to disclose any Evaluation Material or any of the information referred to in paragraph 4 above under the heading “Non-Disclosure of Transaction Discussions” (or if the Company or its Representatives is requested or required to disclose that you are in discussions or negotiations with the Company concerning a possible Transaction) pursuant to any law or regulation or stock exchange rule or regulation or the terms of a valid and effective subpoena or order issued by a court of competent jurisdiction or a federal, state or local governmental or regulatory body or pursuant to a civil investigative demand or similar judicial process or otherwise, the applicable party will, to the extent not prohibited by applicable law or regulation, (i) promptly notify the other party of the existence, terms and circumstances surrounding such request or requirement, (ii) consult with the other party on the advisability of taking legally available steps to resist or narrow such request or requirement, (iii) if disclosure of any such information is required, disclose only that portion of the information which it is legally required to disclose and give the other party notice of the information to be so disclosed as far in advance of disclosure as may be reasonably practicable and (iv) except in the case of public disclosure with respect to any discussions or negotiations concerning a possible Transaction which is required by reason of securities laws or similar requirements related to general disclosure, if so requested by the other party, exercise its commercially reasonable efforts to obtain a protective order or other reliable assurance that confidential treatment will be accorded to such information (and, in any event, if applicable, such party will reasonably cooperate with the other party to obtain such a protective order or other assurance), such efforts to obtain a protective order or confidential treatment to be at the other (requesting) party’s expense.
perturbation.changed_text: If you or your Representatives are requested to disclose any Evaluation Material pursuant to any law, the applicable party will consider notifying the other party and may disclose such information as deemed necessary, giving the other party notice of the information to be so disclosed when feasible. Efforts to obtain a protective order will be assessed based on resource availability.
perturbation.explanation: This change weakens the obligation to notify and consult by replacing 'requested or required' with just 'requested,' removing the obligation to disclose only what is legally required, and introducing subjectivity ('as deemed necessary'). The 'when feasible' and 'assessed based on resource availability' clauses further weaken the obligation to provide advance notice and obtain a protective order.
perturbation.law_explanation: Rule 10b-5 prohibits making untrue statements of material fact or omitting material facts necessary to make statements not misleading. Failing to fully disclose material information under legal obligation or failing to seek a protective order, as the modified clause allows, could be interpreted as withholding necessary information, potentially violating Rule 10b-5 if it involves trading on inside information.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 17 CFR § 240.10b-5 - Employment of manipulative and deceptive devices. | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 17 CFR § 240.10b-5 - Employment of manipulative and deceptive devices. CFR prev | next § 240.10b-5 Employment of manipulative and deceptive devices. It shall be unlawful for any person, directly or indirectly, by the use of any means or instrumentality of interstate commerce, or of the mails or of any facility of any national securities exchange , (a) To employ any device, scheme, or artifice to defraud, (b) To make any untrue statement of a material fact or to omit to state a material fact necessary in order to make the statements made, in the light of the circumstances under which they were made, not misleading, or (c) To engage in any act, practice, or course of business which operates or would operate as a fraud or deceit upon any person, in connection with the purchase or sale of any security . (Sec. 10; 48 Stat. 891 ; 15 U.S.C. 78j ) [ 13 FR 8183 , Dec. 22, 1948, as amended at 16 FR 7928 , Aug. 11, 1951] CFR Toolbox Law about... Articles from Wex Table of Popular Names Parallel Table of Authorities
perturbation.justification: The original text outlines specific actions to be taken when disclosure of information is requested or required by law, including notifying the other party, consulting on resistance, disclosing only what is legally required, and seeking a protective order. The changed text weakens these obligations considerably. It replaces 'requested or required' with just 'requested', removes the requirement to disclose only what is legally required, and introduces subjectivity ('as deemed necessary'). The 'when feasible' and 'assessed based on resource availability' clauses further weaken the obligation to provide advance notice and obtain a protective order. The LLM Law explanation and scraped law snippet 1, specifically 17 CFR § 240.10b-5, address manipulative and deceptive devices including omitting material facts, further indicating that the weakened disclosure obligations could be problematic and create a legal contradiction. The original text's obligatory language is directly undermined, creating ambiguity and potentially leading to non-compliance with disclosure obligations. The modified text creates ambiguity around obligations that were once clearly defined, leading to a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Until the date that is eighteen (18) months from the date of this Agreement, you will not, without the Company’s prior written consent, directly or indirectly solicit for purposes of employment, offer to hire or engage as a consultant, entice away or offer to enter into any contract with, or hire or engage as a consultant or enter into any contract with, during the period of employment or the 90-day period following such employment, any senior or key employee of the Company or its controlled affiliates who becomes known to you in connection with your evaluation of the Transaction; provided , however , that this paragraph will not prohibit you or any of your Representatives from engaging in any general advertising or general solicitation not targeted to any employees or former employees of the Company or its controlled affiliates, or from hiring any employees or former employees of the Company who respond to such solicitation.
perturbation.changed_text: For a period of eighteen (18) months from the date of this Agreement, you will use commercially reasonable judgment not to actively recruit senior or key employees of the Company who become known to you during your evaluation of the Transaction. This does not prevent you from hiring employees who voluntarily apply for positions.
perturbation.explanation: The phrase 'commercially reasonable judgment' is vague and subjective. The original text provided a specific prohibition on solicitation; this change introduces ambiguity about what constitutes 'active recruitment' and allows room for interpretation, potentially undermining the non-solicitation agreement.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New York Non-Compete Law (Although this isn't a non-compete strictly, it is similarly related)
perturbation.law_explanation: While not a direct non-compete, the modified clause weakens the restrictions on employee movement. N.Y. CLS Labor § 201-d protects employees' rights to engage in legal recreational activities outside of work, including seeking employment. If the 'commercially reasonable judgment' clause is interpreted too broadly, it could be argued that it unreasonably restricts employees' ability to pursue employment opportunities, potentially conflicting with the spirit of the law protecting employee freedom.
perturbation.justification: The original text provided a specific prohibition on solicitation, whereas the changed text introduces 'commercially reasonable judgment' regarding active recruitment. This creates ambiguity, as highlighted in the perturbation explanation, undermining the non-solicitation agreement. The Law Explanation suggests that weakening restrictions on employee movement could conflict with employee freedom. Although Scraped Law Snippet 1 does not provide the exact text of N.Y. CLS Labor § 201-d, the Law Explanation suggests the 'commercially reasonable judgment' clause may restrict employees' ability to pursue employment opportunities, creating a legal contradiction as defined by the Ambiguities Legal Contradiction perturbation type.

perturbation.original_text: You acknowledge that money damages and remedies at law may be inadequate to protect the Company against any actual or threatened breach of or failure to comply with this Agreement by you or by your Representatives and, without prejudice to any rights and remedies otherwise available to the Company, you may agree to the seeking of specific performance, injunctive relief and other equitable remedies in the Company’s favor, and you further agree to waive, and to cause your Representatives to waive, any requirement for the securing or posting of any bond in connection with any such remedy.
perturbation.changed_text: You acknowledge that monetary damages may not be the sole remedy for a breach of this Agreement. The Company may consider seeking specific performance, injunctive relief, and other equitable remedies, but the decision to do so is at the Company's discretion. The requirement for securing or posting of any bond in connection with any such remedy shall be subject to further negotiation.
perturbation.explanation: The changes make the seeking of specific performance and injunctive relief discretionary ('may consider seeking') rather than something the other party agrees the company *may* seek. Removing the guarantee to waive the bond requirement ('shall be subject to further negotiation') makes equitable remedies less certain and more difficult to obtain.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New York Civil Practice Law and Rules (CPLR) § 6301
perturbation.law_explanation: NY CPLR § 6301 governs preliminary injunctions. While not explicitly requiring a bond in every case, it outlines the conditions under which an injunction can be granted. By making the waiver of a bond requirement subject to further negotiation, the modified clause introduces uncertainty that may impede the Company's ability to secure an injunction, because the CPLR allows for courts to require a bond before the injunction is granted.

Grounds for preliminary injunction and temporary restraining order. A preliminary injunction may be granted in any action where it appears that the defendant threatens or is about to do, or is doing or procuring or suffering to be done, an act in violation of the plaintiff's rights respecting the subject of the action, and tending to render the judgment ineffectual, or in any action where the plaintiff has demanded and would be entitled to a judgment restraining the defendant from the commission or continuance of an act, which, if committed or continued during the pendency of the action, would produce injury to the plaintiff. A temporary restraining order may be granted pending a hearing for a preliminary injunction where it appears that immediate and irreparable injury, loss or damage will result unless the defendant is restrained before the hearing can be had.
perturbation.justification: The original text ensures the company can seek specific performance, injunctive relief, and other equitable remedies while also guaranteeing the waiver of bond requirements. The changed text makes the seeking of these remedies discretionary and subjects the waiver of bond requirements to further negotiation. This contradicts the intent of NY CPLR § 6301, as stated in the Law Explanation, because the CPLR allows courts to require a bond before an injunction is granted. The change introduces uncertainty regarding the bond, potentially impeding the Company's ability to secure an injunction. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing NY CPLR § 6301, outlines grounds for preliminary injunctions. By removing the guaranteed waiver of the bond, the changed text weakens the company's position regarding injunctive relief, creating a legal contradiction.
